We still retain this idiom in the expression, ‘I would (i. e. wished to) have done it.’ ‘I ought (i. e. was bound) to have done it.’

231.play: With any terms of zeal, wanted the modesty
notes: wanted] CLARENDON: That is, as to have wanted. This lax construction is due to the intervening parenthesis. The following words are grammatically faulty, though the general sense is clear. What man would have been so unreasonably wanting in modesty as to urge you to give up the thing you held as a sacred emblem?
modestie] ALLEN: That is, moderation. Cf. II, ii, 183.

232.play: To urge the thing held as a ceremony?
notes: ceremonie] SCHMIDT (Lex.) finds three significations in which this word is used: First, external form, outward rite; secondly, any thing or observance held sacred [as here]; and thirdly, ritual and solemn performance of a sacred act. CLARENDON: In Hakluyt’s Voyages, quoted by Richardson, a crucifix is called a ceremony.

233.play: Nerissa teaches me what to believe:

234.play: I'll die for't but some woman had the ring.

235.play: BASSANIO: No, by my honour, madam, by my soul,

236.play: No woman had it, but a civil doctor,
notes: ciuill Doctor] A Doctor of Civil Law.

237.play: Which did refuse three thousand ducats of me

238.play: And begg'd the ring; the which I did deny him

239.play: And suffer'd him to go displeased away;

240.play: Even he that did uphold the very life
notes: he] CLARENDON: ‘He’ is used, not him, as if the words ‘the which . . . . away’ were merely parenthetical.

241.play: Of my dear friend. What should I say, sweet lady?

242.play: I was enforced to send it after him;
notes: ALLEN:] It indicates the gentleman and the soldier in Bassanio, that he does not expose Anthonio as the one that ‘enforced’ him to give the ring.

243.play: I was beset with shame and courtesy;

244.play: My honour would not let ingratitude

245.play: So much besmear it. Pardon me, good lady;

246.play: For, by these blessed candles of the night,
notes: Candles] MALONE: The same expression is to be found in the Sonnets, in Macbeth, and in Rom. & Jul.

247.play: Had you been there, I think you would have begg'd
notes: I. thinke] Theobald inserted an unhappy comma after ‘think,’ which has retained its place in the majority of Editions to the present day. White was the first to reject it and restore the original. ‘The difference,’ says WHITE (ed. i), ‘though slight in itself, is material in point of style; for, according to the Folio, “think” has for its subject all the sentence that follows it; but in the text usually given, “I think” is interjectional, and “you would have begged,” &c., is the predication.’ I also prefer ‘And,’ at the beginning of line 241, to For of the Qq.—ED.

248.play: The ring of me to give the worthy doctor.

249.play: PORTIA: Let not that doctor e'er come near my house:

250.play: Since he hath got the jewel that I loved,

251.play: And that which you did swear to keep for me,

252.play: I will become as liberal as you;

253.play: I'll not deny him any thing I have,

254.play: No, not my body nor my husband's bed:

255.play: Know him I shall, I am well sure of it:

256.play: Lie not a night from home; watch me like Argus:

257.play: If you do not, if I be left alone,

258.play: Now, by mine honour, which is yet mine own,

259.play: I'll have that doctor for my bedfellow.

260.play: NERISSA: And I his clerk; therefore be well advised

261.play: How you do leave me to mine own protection.

262.play: GRATIANO: Well, do you so; let not me take him, then;

263.play: For if I do, I'll mar the young clerk's pen.

264.play: ANTONIO: I am the unhappy subject of these quarrels.

265.play: PORTIA: Sir, grieve not you; you are welcome notwithstanding.

266.play: BASSANIO: Portia, forgive me this enforced wrong;

267.play: And, in the hearing of these many friends,

268.play: I swear to thee, even by thine own fair eyes,

271.play: In both my eyes he doubly sees himself;
notes: double] MALONE: Used in a bad sense for—full of duplicity.

272.play: In each eye, one: swear by your double self,

273.play: And there's an oath of credit.

275.play: Pardon this fault, and by my soul I swear

276.play: I never more will break an oath with thee.

277.play: ANTONIO: I once did lend my body for his wealth;
notes: thy] WHITE (ed. i): ‘Thy’ is plainly a corruption. It is possible that it is a misprint for the or that, which had been underlined, and that Shakespeare meant Antonio to say that he lent his body for that happiness (i. e. of both Bassanio and Portia), which else would have miscarried. [This is a plausible suggestion; but I doubt if ‘which’ refers to ‘wealth.’ Does it not refer to the lending, to the pledge, of which Antonio’s body was the forfeit? The happiness of Portia and Bassanio would not have been utterly and forever blasted by Antonio’s death. It was not the wealth or happiness that might have miscarried, but the risk which Antonio himself personally, ran for the sake of Bassanio.—ED.]

wealth] THEOBALD: I have ventured, against the Authority of the Copies, to substitute weal here; i. e. for his welfare, benefit. ‘Wealth’ has a more confined signification. Though I must own that weal and ‘wealth,’ in our author’s time, must be in some measure synonymous; as they are now in the words Common-weal and Commonwealth. JOHNSON: ‘For his wealth’ is—for his advantage; to obtain his happiness. ‘Wealth’ was, at that time, the term opposite to adversity or calamity. STEEVENS: So in The Litany: ‘In all time of our tribulation; in all time of our wealth.’

278.play: Which, but for him that had your husband's ring,

279.play: Had quite miscarried: I dare be bound again,

280.play: My soul upon the forfeit, that your lord
notes: My soule] ALLEN: A dearer pledge than ‘my body.’

281.play: Will never more break faith advisedly.

282.play: PORTIA: Then you shall be his surety. Give him this

283.play: And bid him keep it better than the other.

284.play: ANTONIO: Here, Lord Bassanio; swear to keep this ring.

285.play: BASSANIO: By heaven, it is the same I gave the doctor!

286.play: PORTIA: I had it of him: pardon me, Bassanio;

287.play: For, by this ring, the doctor lay with me.

288.play: NERISSA: And pardon me, my gentle Gratiano;

289.play: For that same scrubbed boy, the doctor's clerk,

290.play: In lieu of this last night did lie with me.
notes: In . . . this] White, accepting ‘in lieu’ in its present use, changed ‘this’ to thee. Halliwell considers the alteration ‘plausible,’ and also attributes the modern meaning to ‘in lieu.’ But Dyce sets them both right by answering the former’s question: ‘What meaning has “in lieu of this”’ here? as follows: ‘It means “in consideration of this (ring).” Compare, earlier in this play, IV, i, 430, “in lieu (i. e. in consideration of) the premises.” And The Tempest, I, ii: “Which was, that he, in lieu o’ (i. e. in consideration of) the premises,—Of homage,” &c.’ And Schmidt (Lex. s. v.) adds four or five more examples. White returned to the original text in his Second Edition.—Ed.

291.play: GRATIANO: Why, this is like the mending of highways

292.play: In summer, where the ways are fair enough:
notes: where] CAPELL (ii, 73): When may appear a proper term in some eyes to follow ‘summer’ than ‘where:’ but ‘where’ heightens the comparison; for, with that, this absurd summer-mending is said, too, to be of ‘ways’ that did not want it at any time. [As White says, this change to when is ‘very plausible, but not necessary.’ The aptness of Gratiano’s simile is a little obscure. I suppose he means that his wife aimed at the purification of his character before it was stained.—Ed.]

293.play: What, are we cuckolds ere we have deserved it?

294.play: PORTIA: Speak not so grossly. You are all amazed:

295.play: Here is a letter; read it at your leisure;

296.play: It comes from Padua, from Bellario:

297.play: There you shall find that Portia was the doctor,

298.play: Nerissa there her clerk: Lorenzo here

299.play: Shall witness I set forth as soon as you

300.play: And even but now return'd; I have not yet
notes: eu’n now] See III, ii, 176. It is not impossible, for grammatical reasons, that this line should be punctuated: ‘And, but e’en now return’d, I have not yet,’ &c.—Ed.

301.play: Enter'd my house. Antonio, you are welcome;

302.play: And I have better news in store for you
notes: ECCLES:] There is not, perhaps, to be found in the dramatic writings of any poet a more lame, awkward [sic], and inartificial expedient for suddenly bringing on a general satisfaction in the catastrophe, than that which is here had recourse to. How Portia should possess the means of acquiring intelligence respecting this happy reverse of Antonio’s fortune earlier than himself, who is just arrived from the very same place, is a matter wonderful to be conceived, and that she should desire to conceal from the knowledge of the company, a circumstance, in its nature so singular and curious, is little less extraordinary.

303.play: Than you expect: unseal this letter soon;

304.play: There you shall find three of your argosies

305.play: Are richly come to harbour suddenly:
notes: richly] See line 46 of this scene.

306.play: You shall not know by what strange accident

309.play: BASSANIO: Were you the doctor and I knew you not?

310.play: GRATIANO: Were you the clerk that is to make me cuckold?

311.play: NERISSA: Ay, but the clerk that never means to do it,

312.play: Unless he live until he be a man.

313.play: BASSANIO: Sweet doctor, you shall be my bed-fellow:

314.play: When I am absent, then lie with my wife.

315.play: ANTONIO: Sweet lady, you have given me life and living;
notes: liuing] STAUNTON: Riches, resources. [See III, ii, 163.]

316.play: For here I read for certain that my ships

319.play: My clerk hath some good comforts too for you.

320.play: NERISSA: Ay, and I'll give them him without a fee.

321.play: There do I give to you and Jessica,

322.play: From the rich Jew, a special deed of gift,

323.play: After his death, of all he dies possess'd of.

324.play: LORENZO: Fair ladies, you drop manna in the way
notes: Manna] Warburton commended the use of this word, as peculiarly appropriate, by Jessica. The unlucky slip did not escape Edwards (Canons of Criticism, p. 212), and he did not waste the chance.
Of ] See ‘I am provided of a Torch-bearer,’ II, iv, 25; ABBOTT, § 171.

327.play: And yet I am sure you are not satisfied

328.play: Of these events at full. Let us go in;
notes: at full] ALLEN: Either, first, equivalent to ‘fully satisfied;’ or, secondly, a confusio duarum locutionum: ‘You are not satisfied [but would like to know of these events] at full. (I lean to the latter.)

329.play: And charge us there upon inter'gatories,
notes: interrogatories] CAMPBELL (p. 62): In the Court of Queen’s Bench, when a complaint is made against a person for a ‘contempt,’ the practice is that before sentence is finally pronounced he is sent into the Crown Office, and being there ‘charged upon interrogatories,’ he is made to swear that he will ‘answer all things faithfully.’

330.play: And we will answer all things faithfully.

331.play: GRATIANO: Let it be so: the first inter'gatory
notes: intergatory] In Praetorius’s Facsimile of Q₁ taken from the Duke of Devonshire’s copy, this word appears as intergory. Another instance, to be added to IV, i, 78, 79, in support of the assumption that this defective copy is an early impression.—Ed.

332.play: That my Nerissa shall be sworn on is,

333.play: Whether till the next night she had rather stay,
notes: Whether] Contracted to whe’r. See WALKER, Vers., p. 103; or ABBOTT, § 466.

334.play: Or go to bed now, being two hours to day:

335.play: But were the day come, I should wish it dark,

336.play: That I were couching with the doctor's clerk.

337.play: Well, while I live I'll fear no other thing
notes: MRS JAMESON (i, 93):] Shylock and his machinations being dismissed from our thoughts, and the rest of the dramatis personae assembled together at Belmont, all our interest and all our attention are riveted on Portia, and the conclusion leaves the most delightful impression on the fancy. The playful equivoque of the rings, the sportive trick she puts on her husband, and her thorough enjoyment of the jest, which she checks just as it is proceeding beyond the bounds of propriety, show how little she was displeased by the sacrifice of her gift, and all are consistent with her bright and joyous spirit. In conclusion, when Portia invites her company to enter her palace to refresh themselves after their travels, and talk over ‘these events at full,’ the imagination, unwilling to lose sight of the brilliant group, follows them in gay procession from the lovely moonlit garden to marble halls and princely revels, to splendour and festive mirth, to love and happiness!

338.play: So sore as keeping safe Nerissa's ring.
